primary_sources.relevance: GitHub describes per-job OIDC JWTs, claim validation, short-lived credentials, RS256 examples, and issuer identity.
GitHub's OIDC claim and subject reference defines the authorization surface downstream of signature verification.
Node exposes standards-compatible fetch used with redirect: manual and AbortController.
HTTP freshness is evaluated using response age and cache-control directives rather than fetch time alone.

finding: Cryptographic verification remains vulnerable to an underspecified metadata transport boundary. Discovery and JWKS retrieval should be pinned to exact HTTPS URLs, reject redirects and final-URL drift, bound time and body size, require JSON, reject stale responses, and perform at most one JWKS refetch only when the selected kid is absent.

cases: redirect rejected with manual redirect mode
unknown kid permits exactly one JWKS refresh
immediately verifiable token performs no refresh
verification.cryptographic_fixture: generated 2048-bit RSA keypair and RS256 compact JWT
verification.note: The local test used a verifier-compatible implementation because direct network retrieval of repository source was unavailable; the committed test targets the repository's existing verifier interface.

uncertainty: No live GitHub-issued OIDC token was tested.
TLS certificate-chain behavior is delegated to the Node runtime and operating system trust store.
The implementation buffers the bounded response after Content-Length precheck; it does not stream-cancel once the byte limit is crossed.
No CI workflow run was observed for the committed test.
falsification_route: Run the committed test against the repository's exact verifier in CI.
Inject 3xx responses, mismatched final URLs, stale Age values, invalid media types, malformed JSON, and bodies above the byte cap.
Demonstrate that more than one JWKS refresh occurs for one verification attempt or that refresh occurs after signature rejection rather than key absence.
Use a live GitHub token in a protected workflow and compare accepted metadata digests and claims with GitHub's published issuer and JWKS.
trust_limit: Accepted metadata transport plus a valid JWT signature does not authorize claims, establish freshness of the token itself, prevent replay, prove a repository mutation, or prove deployment or scientific truth.
